linting for the commit messageIntroduction
If you’ve ever run git log on a mature project and scrolled past commit messages like fix stuff, wip, asdf, or final final v2, you already understand the problem this article solves. A commit history is supposed to be documentation a record of what changed and why. When commit messages are inconsistent, that documentation is worthless, and it quietly costs teams real time: broken changelogs, impossible-to-automate releases, and code reviews that start with "wait, what does this commit actually do?"
Commitlint solves this by enforcing a consistent, machine-readable commit message format at the moment a commit is made not after the fact in a code review comment. Paired with Husky (for Git hooks) and the Conventional Commits specification, it turns commit discipline from a matter of individual willpower into an automated, non-negotiable part of your workflow.
In this guide, we’ll cover:

• What Commitlint is and why it matters

• The Conventional Commits format it enforces

• Step-by-step installation and configuration

• Wiring it up with Husky Git hooks

• Customizing rules for your team

• Enforcing it in CI/CD (for contributors who bypass local hooks)

• Common issues and how to fix them
What Is Commitlint?
Commitlint is a linter conceptually similar to ESLint or Prettier except instead of checking your code, it checks your commit message. When you run git commit, Commitlint intercepts the message via a Git hook, validates it against a defined rule set, and blocks the commit if the message doesn't comply.
It doesn’t touch your code, your branches, or your diffs. Its entire job is to answer one question: “Does this commit message follow the rules we agreed on?”
The typical rule set it enforces is the Conventional Commits specification, though it’s fully configurable if your team wants something custom.
The Conventional Commits Format
Conventional Commits is a lightweight convention on top of commit messages. The structure looks like this
<type>(<scope>): <subject><body>
<footer>

• type the category of change (feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore, perf, ci, build, revert)

• scope (optional) — the part of the codebase affected (auth, api, ui, etc.)

• subject a short, imperative summary of the change

• body (optional) additional context or reasoning

• footer (optional) references to issues, or BREAKING CHANGE: notes
Examples of valid commit messages
feat(auth): add OAuth2 login support
fix(api): handle null response from payment service
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(cart): simplify discount calculation logic
chore: bump dependencies to latest minor versionsWhy this format matters
Once your history follows this structure consistently, it stops being just a history it becomes structured data. That unlocks several things.

• Automated changelogs tools like standard-version or semantic-release can generate a changelog directly from commit history.

• Automated semantic versioning a feat commit can trigger a minor version bump, a fix a patch bump, and a BREAKING CHANGE footer a major bump all without a human deciding the version number.

• Faster code review reviewers instantly know the intent of a commit before reading the diff.

• Easier debugging git log --grep="fix(payment)" becomes an actual useful search.
Prerequisites
Before starting, make sure you have

• A Git repository already initialized (git init if not)

• Node.js and npm (or yarn/pnpm) installed

• A package.json in your project root (npm init -y if you don't have one yet)
Step 1: Install Commitlint
Install the Commitlint CLI along with the Conventional Commits ruleset as dev dependencies.
npm install --save-dev @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventionalUsing yarn
yarn add -D @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventionalUsing pnpm
pnpm add -D @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional
• @commitlint/cli — the command-line tool that actually performs the linting

• @commitlint/config-conventional — a pre-built rule set implementing the Conventional Commits spec, so you don't have to define every rule from scratch
Step 2: Create a Configuration File
Create a commitlint.config.js file in your project root
module.exports = {
extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'],
};You can generate this quickly from the terminal as well
echo "module.exports = { extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'] };" > commitlint.config.jsAt this point, Commitlint is installed and configured but it isn’t wired into your Git workflow yet. That’s where Husky comes in.
Step 3: Install and Initialize Husky
Husky manages Git hooks scripts that run automatically at specific points in the Git lifecycle (pre-commit, commit-msg, pre-push, etc.). We'll use it to trigger Commitlint every time a commit message is written.
npm install --save-dev husky
npx husky inithusky init does two things

• Creates a .husky/ directory in your project

• Adds a prepare script to your package.json so hooks are installed automatically for anyone who runs npm install on the repo
This second point matters it means you don’t have to ask every teammate to manually set up hooks cloning the repo and installing dependencies is enough.
Step 4: Add the commit-msg Hook
Commitlint needs to run specifically on the commit-msg hook, since that's the point in the Git lifecycle where the commit message is available for inspection.
Create the hook file
echo 'npx --no-install commitlint --edit "$1"' > .husky/commit-msgYour .husky/commit-msg file should now contain
npx --no-install commitlint --edit "$1"That’s the entire integration. From this point forward, every git commit will

• Trigger the commit-msg hook

• Pass the commit message to Commitlint

• Validate it against commitlint.config.js

• Block the commit with an error if it doesn’t comply, or let it through silently if it does
Step 5: Test It
Try committing with a message that breaks the rules
git commit -m "fixed stuff"You should see output similar to
⧗ input: fixed stuff
✖ subject may not be end with full stop [subject-full-stop]
✖ type may not be empty [type-empty]
✖ subject may not be empty [subject-empty]✖ found 3 problems, 0 warnings
The commit is rejected before it ever enters your history. Now try a properly formatted message
git commit -m "fix(cart): correct discount calculation for bulk orders"No errors, and the commit proceeds normally.
Step 6: Customizing Rules
The default configuration is a solid starting point, but most teams eventually want to tailor it. You can override or extend individual rules inside commitlint.config.js
module.exports = {
extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'],
rules: {
'type-enum': [
2,
'always',
['feat', 'fix', 'docs', 'style', 'refactor', 'test', 'chore', 'perf', 'ci', 'revert'],
],
'scope-enum': [2, 'always', ['auth', 'api', 'ui', 'db', 'infra']],
'subject-case': [2, 'always', 'lower-case'],
'header-max-length': [2, 'always', 72],
},
};A few notes on the rule format [severity, applicability, value]

• severity 0 disables the rule, 1 shows a warning, 2 throws an error and blocks the commit

• applicability usually 'always' or 'never'

• value the actual constraint (a list of allowed values, a max length, a case style, etc.)
This lets you enforce things like a fixed list of scopes matching your project’s actual modules, or a maximum subject line length to keep git log --oneline readable.
Step 7: Enforce It in CI (Don’t Rely on Local Hooks Alone)
Git hooks live locally. A contributor can bypass them with git commit --no-verify, or simply not have hooks installed correctly. If commit message quality actually matters to your project, add a CI check as a second line of defense.
In a GitHub Actions workflow, you can lint every commit in a pull request
name: Lint Commit Messageson: [pull_request]
jobs:
commitlint:
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
with:
fetch-depth: 0
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
with:
node-version: 20
- run: npm install
- run: npx commitlint --from ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }} --to ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }} --verbose
This validates every commit in the pull request range, not just the local ones closing the gap left by bypassable local hooks.
Wrapping Up
Commitlint’s real value isn’t the linting itself it’s what a disciplined commit history unlocks downstream automated changelogs, automated semantic versioning, and a project history that actually tells a story. The setup is minimal two packages, one config file, one Git hook but the payoff compounds over the life of a project, especially as team size grows and “just remember to write good commit messages” stops being a realistic policy.
If you’re introducing this to an existing project, don’t be afraid to start with just @commitlint/config-conventional and no custom rules. You can always tighten the rule set later once the team is comfortable with the format.
